item.title: Everest MiCA CASP — 72 replies — 288.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Everest MiCA CASP

Open thread • Replies: 72 • Updated: 2026-07-15 15:02 UTC

OP

I want to personally thank every Everest ID hater in here, talking to you Link baggies. They secured MiCA CASP last week.

A single MFSA license is valid across 27 EU states, reaching ~450M people. Of the ~283 CASPs on the ESMA register, nearly all are exchanges or brokers. Everest is one of the rare licensed infrastructure providers: identity and payment rails, not another venue.

Enjoy it furries

Early replies (first 3)

62461998
>>62461988
I thought it would have mooned harder from the news. What gives

62462006
It is because we are meant to suffer as token holders just like the Linkies

62463131
>Unlisted from coingecko
Lmao!

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62472827
>>62472822
• Stellar TVL and volume surge from DTCC flows XLM utility and price re-rate significantly (institutional adoption narrative goes parabolic).
• Everest, as an early regulated builder with SDF backing, sees user growth, revenue from services, and potential token ($ID) or valuation upside as the “Stellar-compliant layer in Europe.”
• Multi-chain expansion (already hinting BTC/SOL) positions them beyond one network while Stellar remains the core for real-world compliant assets.
• Timing: 2026–2027 window is when pilots go live. Everest’s Kickstart work is shipping now, giving them first-mover polish in wallets/UI.
Why They Chose Stellar “For a Reason”: Low costs + speed for payments/remittances, built-in compliance primitives perfect for regulated tokenized securities, and now direct DTCC pipeline. Ethereum is great for DeFi but heavier/costlier for high-volume settlement. Stellar is the efficient, institutional-friendly public chain.
In the super scenario: DTCC/Stellar becomes the standard for tokenized securities. Everest rides it as the go-to EU-compliant wallet/custody/payments layer. XLM reprices for infrastructure status. Everest scales into a major fintech player in the tokenized economy, with strong revenue, user base, and ecosystem gravity.
This is the aligned, non-random bet: regulation + infrastructure + timing + grants + institutional tailwinds. High conviction setup if the DTCC integration delivers.

Building Where Demand Is Real
One of the clearest signals we saw in 2025 was growing traction in the foreign exchange and cross-border payments markets. Across multiple conversations with providers, a consistent need emerged: the ability to compliantly onboard users and rapidly convert fiat currencies into USD-backed stablecoins, without friction or regulatory ambiguity.
Everest worked to address this need. During the year, we supported and tested flows involving EUR, GBP, NOK, BRL, and other fiat currencies, converting into USD-denominated stablecoin rails with speed, auditability, and compliance front-and-center. For FX and payments providers, speed matters — but compliance matters more.

item.title: Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK? — 51 replies — 204.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK?

Open thread • Replies: 51 • Updated: 2026-07-15 15:02 UTC

OP

Every time I see the UK mentioned here, it is always in a negative light.

It is always
>In the USA, wages are better!
>In Europe, houses are cheaper!
>The FTSE is underperforming!
>My taxes are too high!

There is never any mention of the fantastic benefits which come with life in the UK. The fantastic healthcare for minimal cost, the great education system available to everybody, the dynamic modern economy with many bustling sectors, and everything else.

Taxes are higher than the USA but they are lower than most of Europe and we still enjoy fantastic public services whereas in America there are no public services or safety nets.

Not to mention the things you cannot measure. Being able to walk the streets in safety without fear of gun violence and gangs. Enjoying the fantastic beautiful nature and gardens. Living in the historical majesty and balancing that with modern cosmopolitan qualities.

The UK might not be the best place to become rich but nobody is poor here and it is very easy to enjoy life if you have goals beyond the number in your bank account.

Early replies (first 3)

62481973
>>62481958
the problem is that its being filled with immigrants who are living off tax payers money
thats the gist of why everyone is so pissed off here

62481986
>>62481973
So just right wing racebaiting?

No, I think it runs deeper than that. Most people have not personally had negative experiences with migrants, and reading the Daily Mail does not count.

I am trying to angle from a financial perspective too. People talk about the UK like it is a poor country but it is one of the richest countries on the planet. I think perhaps they are just spoiled and do not know what poor countries are really like. I blame social media.

62481994
>>62481958
>fantastic healthcare for minimal cost
my nan lived in birmingham and she came down with leukaemia and was gone within a couple of months. She never even got to see a doctor because the muslim immigrants were getting seen first. if i had something to lose i wouldnt even dare post this for fear of legal reprimand.

62483373
Mockery coming from Americans and Canadians are justified because those countries still offer much better living standards for middle and working class people and a higher level of personal liberties, although Canada is obviously on a downward trend.

Europeans (except for Switzerland and Norway) can't say shit about us. I don't care what cope statistics they pull out of their arses. Eurostat is fake and gay. I've lived in Europe and Britain is a capitalist paradise compared to them. That's why in the days of free movement it was Eurofaggots moving here and not the other way around. They get taxed to shit. Most EU countries don't have a tax free personal allowance. Most of them don't even have anything similar to a stock and shares ISA. A shocking number of them have CGT on unrealised gains. Europoors can't even withdraw a tax free lump sum from their retirement pots and they all have regulations forcing providers to invest in local businesses so when a French pays into his private pension pot which by the way, most people don't even have one since they have very little left to invest after their insane income tax levels, he won't be getting S&P500 level returns, his money will go straight into Louis Vuitton stocks or something gay like that.

They will talk shit about our economy's over reliance on the financial sector while ignoring the fact a dozen European countries still rely on tourism. Fucking tourism, like some Caribbean shithole.

And the way their "common market" works leads to some pretty wild and grossly inaccurate numbers that inflate their income levels. Every book sold by Amazon in the EU is officially sold by Luxembourg. Most tech companies are based in Ireland which gives them a hilarious $140k GDP per capita. Anyone who's been to Dublin can tell they're much poorer than that. Their wealthiest city looks like a gentrified Sheffield and we're supposed to believe they're the 3rd wealthiest people on the planet.

62483377
UK was cool 2500 years ago.

62483416
>>62483373
Look! He's trying to make out that England isn't a sick, cringeworthy country for the mentally retarded!

62483981
>>62481958
UK used to be a S tier zone, now it's more like an A- or a B+ zone. I'm from Eastern Europe and if I moved to UK it 100% wouldn't be worth it economically, would most likely be a downgrade in living conditions.
So Eastern Europe living conditions with mass migrants on top.

I'm a retard on this topic but my guess is that there has been fuck all investments in the UK since 1980s, because the big capital had a preference for asian and african regions for both ideological and profit reasons. No investments + exclusively extractory business practices for ~40 years will bring such results. So economically UK is like an old, neglected region that stagnated so hard that even Eastern Europe (that was rekt by 50 years of communism) is becoming a better place to live. Certainly a much better place to settle down and start a family.
Culturally it's a sad joke, a parody of its former self.

It's not even worth it to visit UK for a few years to accumulate savings, because it offers you no such prospects. UK has literally nothing to offer to Europeans. And most bongs would be better off somewhere else. It's in a very weird position right now, and degrading rapidly.

62483989

404 not found
>Being able to walk the streets in safety without fear of gun violence and gangs
Oh ok this is a parody post
>if you have goals beyond the number in your bank account.
Other words for: If you don't mind being poor and never making it

62484019
Ireland is better in every possible way. Brits are a miserable bunch.

62484139
>>62483373
>>62483373
Quick comparison.

UK:
In my sector I should be able to land a 90 000 £ / year job in the UK. That's about 5200 £ per month after taxes. For my family the living expenses (rent, bills, council tax, food, everyday expenses) would total to about ~4200 £ a month, so would be able to live reasonably comfortably and save 1000 £ a month.

Eastern Europe:
In Eastern Europe I can land a 5000 £ / month job, 4000 £ after taxes. Same expenses around here would cost around 2000 £ a month. So I can save twice as much as in the UK.

Now let's compare it after 5, 10 and 15 years of savings with +8% per year.

UK: 5/10/15 - 73k / 182 / 346 £
EE: 5/10/15 - 146k / 365k / 692k £

UK is a huge downgrade from Eastern Europe. And that's regardless of the old, failing infrastructure and mass migration.

62484232
>>62481958
You think being exposed to sunlight, living in a house and having white teeth is so abnormal you made a meme about and called them deanos.

62484259
>>62484139
"Eastern Europe" goes from Poland all the way to fucking Albania so stop being an obtuse nigger (I notice every single one of you are when bashing the UK) and specify what country you're talking about. Then we can have a look at
1- How many jobs are there paying the equivalent to £5,000/month. This is very easy to estimate when you use local firms CEO salaries as benchmark, which are generally publicly available in listed companies.
2- The actual tax brackets of the country you're talking about (there's no "Eastern European" income tax)
3- How much CGT you'd be paying on those savings. I think you meant investment because 8% return on savings would only happen in a country raped by very high inflation. It would be zero in the UK btw as £1,000/month would keep you well below the ISA allowance.

item.title: It's Over: Zcash (ZEC) has defeated Monero (XMR) — 7 replies — 28.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
It's Over: Zcash (ZEC) has defeated Monero (XMR)

Open thread • Replies: 7 • Updated: 2026-07-15 15:05 UTC

OP

I give up. It's over. With the news that zcash will eventually eliminate the transparent pool (see link below) it has utterly defeated the last argument that Monero ever had against it.

The argument against optional privacy was a pretty good one. Privacy shouldn't be something you have to opt into. Now zcash has patched up even that argument.

So what do you have? You have an extremely well funded coin with mandatory privacy, on all exchanges, with all the marketing and engineering budget to expand forever. Jews in support. Institutions coming. MSTR holding companies. Price skyrocketing. KOLs. Lobbyists. And the ONLY arguments against it are spurious and hard to prove. "Muh it has a backdoor" (can't be demonstrated) "Muh jews" (nobody cares) "Muh VCs" (nobody cares).

In comparison Monero is entirely self funded. Barely enough budget to keep its ~10 devs employed. No marketing at all. The only "success" it has is being used for acquiring fucking drugs on a couple of shady marketplaces nobody has ever heard of.

I loved what Monero stood for and I thought that investing in privacy would be a life changing financial decision. What actually happened was Jews stole your momentum, used it for themselves, adopted ALL of your tech and design decisions, ALL of your memes, and enriched and empowered themselves. Meanwhile you pretend your bazaar or drug market makes you superior. You, the loser goy, are destined to this life of serfdom and hardship. I thought I was amongst people who understood the reality of power. Apparently not.

https://x.com/Zcash/status/2077150354505118014

>$581 ZEC vs $332 XMR and climbing

I'm a professional making 6 figs a year in an white collar position, I use LLMs every single day outside of work and inside of work.

62483326
I have become so accustomed to laidback NEET like living that i don't know if i even have the attention span to focus for 8 hours of work every day

62483345
>>62483326
Depending on your early life and early adulthood, maybe.
Hopefully you did some difficult things when you were a kid/teenager and showed up to school on time. Showing up is the first step to working, and a history of showing up is what will help an employer believe you will be there 5 days a week (the first step to getting hired to a good job.)
If you're still a young adult and not too far removed from your school days, you still have all those years of waking up and making it to class in your habits.
Hopefully you did things like long hard hikes, picking fruit all day for cash, biking around for hours at a time trying to keep up with the older kids, reading long novels in binge sessions (this one's a push, but it is maybe some hope.) Doing any of this may be an indicator you have some push in you. Hopefully you do even if you haven't pushed yourself much.

There are jobs where you barely need to focus. The work comes at you, and you do it. If you just shrug it off the supervisors will give you some pushes, and if you don't answer the calls, serve the customers, pick the weeds, you'll be let go.
You might think landscaping is so bad, but you get driven around by the crew boss, maybe a couple funny lads to joke around with, and you just gotta get it done when it's time to unload and work. Back in the truck and off to the next job. It's broken up, it's not so bad. You shed weight, get a lot of sun and hopefully some chipper lads to laugh with.

If going to work doesn't risk your benefits, you could give it a try. For young people (24 and under) fastfood can be great. You'll be around people like you all day, and being close to people is the number one way to develop relationships. It's not always busy, but they'll always have something for you to be doing, which may challenge your attention. Specific advice is higher up in the thread with more suggestions of workplaces

62481649
The oil industry goes through cycles. When prices are high, it makes it economically profitable to drill new wells and then everyone starts pumping as much as they can to make as much money as they can. If economic growth is good, this can create a balance between the oil being produced and the growth fueling the economy, but if growth starts to lag or if oil production simply outpaces it, then there starts to be a glut and oil prices drop.

This is where were were at in the cycle prior to the Iran war. When this happens, the cost to run many wells exceeds what they can get from the oil so they have to shut them down. Then the glut gets worked through, prices go back up and the cycle begins anew.

The Iran war has massively distorted the normal cycles. The glut we were supposed to work through slowly and steadily as wells went offline here and there has been burned through. Oil producers got higher prices for a while allowing them to maintain output at profitable levels for a while longer, but the gulf states got fucked. They will have to start capping wells because they have nowhere to put their oil seeing as how it can't get out of the strait.

Strategic reserves will have to be replenished after this, so prices will remain high as government replenish their reserves.

The one unanswered question here is how much of the glut prior to all this came from a faltering economy. If the economy was already bad, then having the glut burned through quickly combined with strategic reserves buying, the price of oil will be too high to support the economic recovery that will be needed. The fact that the price keeps going down again no matter the supply shocks suggests there is actually demand destruction on a massive scale, probably because China is having a recession. That will drag down the global economy into a recession if it hasn't already.

>>62480300
The Clarity Act is important because it would provide a much clearer regulatory framework for cryptocurrency and establish clearer rules for the industry. That legal certainty is one of the biggest reasons many financial institutions have been cautious about fully embracing blockchain technology and tokenizing real-world assets. Especially since you see so many scams going on, and those influencers never see the inside of a jail cell. It makes it harder for businesses and banks to take crypto seriously and makes them hesitant because if the SEC decides to act against them, there are no clear rules to protect them from liability. Companies managing billions of dollars aren’t going to want to invest in new tech because, again, there are no clear rules. If the act passes then more institutions will feel comfortable using blockchain technology.
